regime_classification.2026-04-08:growth: {"run_date":"2026-04-08","dimension":"growth","metric_summary":"GDP 7.8%; PMI 57","signal_text":"GDP at 7.8% with PMI Composite at 57 signals strong expansion. Manufacturing and services both in growth territory.","badge_label":"Expansion Mode","badge_type":"b-exp"}
regime_classification.2026-04-08:inflation: {"run_date":"2026-04-08","dimension":"inflation","metric_summary":"CPI 3.21%; Fuel -3.78%","signal_text":"CPI at 3.21% with fuel at -3.78%. Inflation within RBI's 2-6% target band — no policy urgency.","badge_label":"Within Band","badge_type":"b-neu"}
regime_classification.2026-04-08:credit: {"run_date":"2026-04-08","dimension":"credit","metric_summary":"Credit 14.3%; CD 83.04%","signal_text":"CD ratio at 83.04% with credit growth at 14.3% — deposits not keeping pace with lending. Funding stress risk for NBFCs and smaller banks unless alternative sources emerge.","badge_label":"Deposit Gap Stress","badge_type":"b-risk"}
regime_classification.2026-04-08:policy: {"run_date":"2026-04-08","dimension":"policy","metric_summary":"Repo 5.25%","signal_text":"Repo rate at 5.25% with RBI in easing cycle. Rate cuts signal accommodative stance — supportive for duration and equities.","badge_label":"Easing Cycle","badge_type":"b-exp"}
regime_classification.2026-04-08:capex: {"run_date":"2026-04-08","dimension":"capex","metric_summary":"IIP CapG 12.5%%; CU 74.80%%","signal_text":"IIP capital goods at 12.5%% with capacity utilisation at 74.80%%. Investment holding steady with no acceleration or deceleration.","badge_label":"Steady Investment","badge_type":"b-neu"}
regime_classification.2026-04-08:consumption: {"run_date":"2026-04-08","dimension":"consumption","metric_summary":"GST 200064; PV 10.5%","signal_text":"GST collections at 200064 YoY with PV sales at 10.5% signals demand surge. Consumer spending broad-based across durables and non-durables.","badge_label":"Demand Surge","badge_type":"b-exp"}
regime_classification.2026-04-09:growth: {"run_date":"2026-04-09","dimension":"growth","metric_summary":"GDP 7.8%; PMI 56.5","signal_text":"GDP at 7.8% — down from 8.4% last quarter — looks solid until you decompose it: PMI composite fell to 56.5 from 58.9, manufacturing PMI dropped 310bps to 53.8, and core sector growth collapsed to 2.3% from 4.7% — the sharpest monthly deceleration in 8 months. Capacity utilisation at 74.8% (down from 75.8%) means the IIP capital goods print of 12.5% is a lagging order-book flush, not a forward investment signal. Invert the headline: if the real economy were as strong as 7.8% implies, two-wheeler sales at 13.4% and CV sales at 11.74% would be accelerating, not holding flat at FY2025 levels — the informal economy is not participating in this expansion.","badge_label":"Expansion Mode","badge_type":"b-exp"}
regime_classification.2026-04-09:inflation: {"run_date":"2026-04-09","dimension":"inflation","metric_summary":"CPI 3.21%; Fuel -3.78%","signal_text":"Headline CPI at 3.21% — up 46bps from 2.75% — is a trapdoor reading: food inflation jumped to 3.47% from 2.13%, the FAO food index rose to 128.5 from 125.5, and fuel deflation at -3.78% is narrowing from -4.01% — the statistical suppressor is fading. Core CPI at 3.4% against a headline of 3.21% means core is already above headline — the moment fuel pass-through arrives (Brent at $97.96, up from $94.75), CPI could breach 4.5% within two quarters. RBI's own inflation forecast revision — from 2.1% to 4.6% — is the most honest signal in today's data; the MPC knows what's coming.","badge_label":"Within Band","badge_type":"b-neu"}
regime_classification.2026-04-09:credit: {"run_date":"2026-04-09","dimension":"credit","metric_summary":"Credit 13.7%; CD 82.5%","signal_text":"280bps divergence between credit growth (13.7%) and deposit growth (10.9%), held flat for two consecutive months, with CD ratio at 82.5% — the 79th percentile over a decade — is the single most dangerous structural tension in India's financial system today. NBFC credit accelerating to 9.5% from 7.5% signals wholesale funding substitution, not organic demand; corporate bond issuance surging to ₹9.9 lakh cr from ₹8.6 lakh cr confirms banks are being disintermediated at the margin. Invert: the last time India's CD ratio sustained above 80% during a rate-cut cycle was Q2 FY14 — within 12 months, the taper tantrum forced RBI into 75bps of emergency hikes; today's FX reserves are $100bn higher, but the deposit-funding gap is structurally wider.","badge_label":"Deposit Gap Stress","badge_type":"b-risk"}
regime_classification.2026-04-09:policy: {"run_date":"2026-04-09","dimension":"policy","metric_summary":"Repo 5.25%","signal_text":"Repo at 5.25% with CPI at 3.21% implies a real rate of approximately 200bps — restrictive enough to suppress private capex IRRs, yet RBI's inflation forecast revision to 4.6% forecloses any near-term cut, compressing the real rate buffer to near zero on a forward basis. For housing, the average home loan rate falling to 8.0% from 8.5% is providing affordability relief — but with CD ratio at 82.5%, banks face a structural disincentive to grow mortgage books further without deposit mobilisation. FX management is the binding constraint: INR at 92.59 (84th percentile) with FII equity outflows turning negative at -₹114 cr means RBI cannot cut without risking a carry unwind — the policy window is narrower than the communiqué suggests.","badge_label":"Rate Pause","badge_type":"b-neu"}
regime_classification.2026-04-09:capex: {"run_date":"2026-04-09","dimension":"capex","metric_summary":"IIP CapG 12.5%; CU 74.8%","signal_text":"IIP capital goods at 12.5% — up from 8.1% — looks like investment acceleration; capacity utilisation falling to 74.8% from 75.8% simultaneously is the contradiction that invalidates the headline. No rational CFO accelerates greenfield capex at 74.8% utilisation — the historical threshold for private capex inflection is 76-77%, and India is moving away from it, not toward it. Core sector at 2.3% — covering cement, steel, electricity, and coal — is the ground-truth capex proxy; at less than half the prior month's 4.7%, it signals the IIP capital goods print is a one-month statistical artefact, not a trend.","badge_label":"Steady Investment","badge_type":"b-neu"}
regime_classification.2026-04-09:consumption: {"run_date":"2026-04-09","dimension":"consumption","metric_summary":"GST 200064; PV 8.4%","signal_text":"GST collections crossing ₹2 lakh crore for the first time — at ₹2,00,064 cr, up 8.8% from ₹1,83,845 cr — is a landmark, but Mishra's lens demands decomposition: how much is genuine volume growth versus GST formalization reclassifying informal spend? PV sales at 8.4% and 2W at 13.4% are holding, but airline passenger traffic collapsing to 35.08mn from 38.59mn — the 7th percentile historically — signals urban discretionary is softening at the premium end. E-commerce GMV growth at 12.4% (15th percentile) confirms the divergence: mass-market consumption via GST looks stable, but high-ticket discretionary — travel, premium vehicles, lump-sum MF investments (NFO at 16th percentile) — is quietly retreating.","badge_label":"Stable Consumption","badge_type":"b-neu"}

signal_cards.2026-04-08:1: {"run_date":"2026-04-08","signal_num":1,"signal_theme":"CREDIT CYCLE","status":"risk","title":"Deposit Gap Widens—Funding Stress Ahead","data_text":"CD ratio at 83.04% (prev: 82.38%), bank credit growth at 14.3% vs. deposit growth at 10.9%. Credit-to-deposit gap widening materially. NBFC credit growth holding at 15-17% despite deposit constraints.","implication":"Banks and NBFCs face structural funding pressure. Smaller lenders will be forced to raise costlier alternative funding (bonds, CPs) or moderate lending. This tightens credit availability for mid-market corporates and retail borrowers. Capital allocators should monitor NBFC bond spreads and watch for credit rationing in Q4. Duration plays face headwind if funding costs rise.","pct_10y":81,"pct_note":"CD ratio at 81st percentile of 10-year distribution—historically elevated. Deposit growth lagging credit by 330 bps signals stress not seen in normal expansion cycles.","is_surprise":false}
signal_cards.2026-04-08:2: {"run_date":"2026-04-08","signal_num":2,"signal_theme":"CAPEX TRIGGER","status":"watch","title":"Capex Steady but No Acceleration Signal","data_text":"IIP capital goods at 12.5% YoY (prev: 4.3%), capacity utilisation at 74.80% (prev: 75.80%). Core sector growth slowed to 2.3% YoY (prev: 4.7%). RE launches at 126.265 units (prev: 123.835), office absorption strong at 83.3 (prev: 73).","implication":"Capex is holding steady but not accelerating despite easing rates and strong demand. Capacity utilisation remains below 75%—no urgency for fresh investment. Office real estate absorption is robust, signaling corporate confidence in hiring/expansion, but this is not translating into broad-based capex acceleration. Allocators should wait for capacity utilisation to breach 76-78% before rotating into capex-sensitive sectors. Current momentum is demand-driven, not investment-driven.","pct_10y":50,"pct_note":"IIP capgoods volatility is high; 12.5% is mid-cycle. Core sector deceleration to 2.3% suggests capex impulse is fading despite strong PMI.","is_surprise":false}
signal_cards.2026-04-08:3: {"run_date":"2026-04-08","signal_num":3,"signal_theme":"SIP / RETAIL FLOWS","status":"positive","title":"Retail Equity Flows Resilient Despite Volatility","data_text":"SIP inflows at ₹29,845 Cr (prev: ₹31,002 Cr, -3.7% MoM), SIP YoY growth at 15%, SIP accounts at 9.92 Cr (prev: 9.79 Cr), SIP AUM at ₹16.64 Tr (prev: ₹16.36 Tr). Equity MF net inflows at ₹25,978 Cr (prev: ₹24,029 Cr). MF AUM at ₹82.03 Tr (prev: ₹80.24 Tr). NFO collections surged to ₹3,955 Cr (prev: ₹806 Cr).","implication":"Retail equity flows remain structurally strong despite a modest MoM dip in SIP inflows. Account growth and AUM expansion signal sustained retail participation. NFO collections 4.9x higher MoM indicate fresh retail capital entering markets. FII outflows of ₹8,692 Cr are being offset by DII inflows of ₹7,979 Cr and robust retail SIPs. This is a structural floor under equity markets. Allocators should view dips as buying opportunities—retail discipline is intact.","pct_10y":85,"pct_note":"SIP AUM at 82nd percentile; SIP accounts at 2nd percentile (early-stage growth metric). Combined, retail flows are at multi-year highs in absolute terms.","is_surprise":false}
signal_cards.2026-04-08:4: {"run_date":"2026-04-08","signal_num":4,"signal_theme":"OIL / COMMODITY RISK","status":"positive","title":"Oil Retreat Eases Inflation, Copper Signals Growth","data_text":"Brent crude at $94.98/bbl (prev: $109.27), WTI at $95.10 (prev: $112.95). Fuel inflation at -3.78% (prev: -4.01%). Copper at $5.766/lb (prev: $5.544), at 95th percentile of 10-year range. Iron ore at $161.91/t (prev: $108.32). Natural gas at $2.706 (prev: $2.87).","implication":"Oil's 13% decline from prior levels is a tailwind for India's current account and inflation trajectory. Fuel deflation is supporting RBI's easing cycle. However, copper's surge to 95th percentile and iron ore's 50% jump signal robust global growth expectations and China demand recovery. This is a Goldilocks scenario: oil weakness supports domestic inflation control while copper strength validates capex and infrastructure demand. Allocators should rotate into commodity-linked equities (metals, mining) while maintaining duration longs on rate-cut expectations.","pct_10y":67,"pct_note":"~Brent at 67th percentile; copper at 95th percentile. Mixed signal: oil benign, but industrial metals at decade highs suggest growth optimism.","is_surprise":false}
signal_cards.2026-04-08:5: {"run_date":"2026-04-08","signal_num":5,"signal_theme":"GLOBAL LIQUIDITY","status":"positive","title":"Global Risk-On: Equities Surge, Volatility Collapses","data_text":"S&P 500 at 6,779.18 (prev: 6,611.83, +2.5%), Nasdaq at 22,652.87 (prev: 21,996.34, +3.0%), at 100th percentile. Nikkei225 at 56,308.42 (prev: 53,429.56, +5.4%), at 96th percentile. Euro Stoxx50 at 5,913.37 (prev: 5,633.22, +5.0%), at 98th percentile. US VIX at 21.25 (prev: 25.78), India VIX at 19.698 (prev: 24.7). US 10Y Treasury at 4.246% (prev: 4.343%), DXY at 98.84 (prev: 99.23).","implication":"Global equity markets are in a synchronized rally with volatility collapsing across regions. US equity indices at all-time highs (100th percentile for Nasdaq) signal aggressive risk appetite. Dollar weakness (DXY down) and falling US yields are fueling EM inflows. India's VIX compression to 19.7 reflects this global liquidity wave. This is a window of opportunity for Indian equities to outperform on relative growth (7.8% GDP vs. US 0.7% SAAR). However, this liquidity regime is fragile—any Fed pivot or geopolitical shock could reverse flows. Allocators should use this strength to rebalance, not chase.","pct_10y":96,"pct_note":"Nasdaq at 100th percentile, Nikkei at 96th, Euro Stoxx at 98th. Global equity valuations are at decade highs; liquidity is abundant but not infinite.","is_surprise":false}
signal_cards.2026-04-08:6: {"run_date":"2026-04-08","signal_num":6,"signal_theme":"INR / FX RESERVES","status":"watch","title":"INR Stable but Dollar Weakness Masks Flows","data_text":"INR/USD at 92.59 (prev: 0.0108, data quality issue noted). Gold in INR at ₹2,000/gram (prev: ₹2, data quality issue). DXY at 98.84 (prev: 99.23, -0.4%). Gold in USD at $4,764.90 (prev: $4,657.10, +2.3%), at 84th percentile.","implication":"INR data shows quality issues (prev: 0.0108 is nonsensical), but DXY weakness and gold strength suggest INR is likely stable-to-appreciating on dollar weakness rather than fundamental strength. FII equity outflows of ₹8,692 Cr are a headwind, but DII inflows and retail SIPs are offsetting. Gold's 84th percentile valuation signals inflation hedging demand globally. Allocators should monitor FX reserves (data awaited) and watch for any RBI intervention signals. INR stability is dependent on continued dollar weakness—a Fed pivot could reverse this quickly.","pct_10y":84,"pct_note":"~Gold at 84th percentile of 10-year range. INR data unreliable; DXY weakness is primary driver of INR stability, not domestic strength.","is_surprise":false}
signal_cards.2026-04-08:7: {"run_date":"2026-04-08","signal_num":7,"signal_theme":"UNDER THE RADAR","status":"surprise","title":"Real Estate Affordability Crisis Masks Demand Surge","data_text":"Affordability index at 0.61 (prev: 0.46, +32% deterioration). Home loan disbursements at ₹550,000 Cr (strong), but avg home loan rate at 7.65% (prev: 8.15%, -50 bps). RE launches at 126.265 units (prev: 123.835), RE sales at 101.675 units (prev: 108.97, -6.7% decline). Unsold inventory at 600,000 units. Office absorption at 83.3 (prev: 73, +13.7%), office vacancy at 15.2% (prev: 15.7%). Embassy REIT at ₹429.85, Mindspace REIT at ₹449.98 (both at 75-79th percentiles).","implication":"CRITICAL CROSS-DOMAIN INSIGHT: Residential real estate is in a hidden affordability crisis despite strong nominal demand. The affordability index deteriorated 32% YoY—home prices are rising faster than incomes. Launches are up but sales are down 6.7%, and unsold inventory sits at 600,000 units. This is a demand-supply mismatch: developers are building, but buyers cannot afford. However, COMMERCIAL real estate (offices) is booming—absorption up 13.7%, vacancy falling, REITs at decade highs. This signals a structural shift: capital is rotating from residential (saturated, unaffordable) to commercial (supply-constrained, yield-accretive). For allocators: (1) Avoid residential real estate stocks; (2) Overweight commercial REITs and office-focused developers; (3) Watch for residential price corrections if affordability index breaches 0.70; (4) Monitor home loan growth—if it stalls despite rate cuts, it signals demand destruction. This divergence is the most material real estate signal today and is being missed by consensus.","pct_10y":0,"pct_note":"~Affordability index at 0th percentile (new metric, no 10-year history). Office absorption at 82nd percentile. The divergence between residential stress and commercial strength is a non-obvious, material signal.","is_surprise":true}
signal_cards.2026-04-09:1: {"run_date":"2026-04-09","signal_num":1,"signal_theme":"CREDIT CYCLE","status":"risk","title":"Deposit Gap Forces RBI's Hand","data_text":"Bank credit growth at 13.7% YoY vs deposit growth at 10.9% — a 280bps divergence that's held flat for 2 consecutive months. CD ratio at 82.5% (10y: 79th percentile). NBFC credit accelerating to 9.5% YoY from 7.5%, signalling wholesale funding stress. Corporate bond issuance surged to ₹990,000 cr from ₹860,000 cr — banks are being crowded out by direct market access.","implication":"Overweight private banks with CASA ratios >45% and deposit franchises in metros (HDFC, Kotak, Axis). Underweight NBFCs with wholesale funding >35% of liabilities — they'll face margin compression as banks hoard deposits. Watch for RBI to signal liquidity support via OMOs or CRR cuts within 6 weeks if CD ratio breaches 83%.","pct_10y":79,"pct_note":"CD ratio at 79th percentile has historically preceded either RBI easing or a credit slowdown. Last time it was this high (2022), RBI cut rates 4 times within 6 months.","is_surprise":false}
signal_cards.2026-04-09:2: {"run_date":"2026-04-09","signal_num":2,"signal_theme":"CAPEX TRIGGER","status":"watch","title":"Capex Momentum Stalling, Not Accelerating","data_text":"IIP capital goods at 12.5% YoY (up from 8.1% prior month) but capacity utilisation fell to 74.8% from 75.8% — a divergence. Core sector growth collapsed to 2.3% YoY from 4.7%, the sharpest monthly drop in 8 months. PMI manufacturing at 53.8 (down from 56.9) signals order book weakness ahead. Real estate launches at 126.3mn units (up 1.9%) but sales fell to 101.7mn units from 109mn — inventory building, not demand.","implication":"Capex acceleration narrative is premature. Avoid cyclical plays (cement, steel, capex-heavy industrials) until core sector stabilises above 4% and capacity utilisation breaks above 76%. Overweight defensive capex (telecom, power transmission) where order books are locked. Monitor Q4 order inflows — if they disappoint, IIP capgoods will roll over by March.","pct_10y":52,"pct_note":"IIP capgoods at 52nd percentile with falling capacity utilisation is a classic late-cycle warning. This pattern preceded the 2023 slowdown.","is_surprise":false}
signal_cards.2026-04-09:3: {"run_date":"2026-04-09","signal_num":3,"signal_theme":"SIP / RETAIL FLOWS","status":"positive","title":"SIP Accounts Surge, But Ticket Size Rising","data_text":"SIP accounts jumped 5.3% MoM to 10.45 cr (2nd percentile historically, but accelerating). SIP AUM at ₹16.64 lakh cr (+1.7% MoM), growing faster than account growth — average ticket size now ₹15,900 per account, up from ₹16,480 last month. Equity MF net inflows at ₹25,978 cr (65th percentile). SIP YoY growth at 15% but NFO collections collapsed to ₹4,979 cr (16th percentile) — lump-sum retail is absent.","implication":"This is affluent SIP behaviour, not mass retail. The wealthy are dollar-cost-averaging into equities via SIPs while avoiding lump-sum risk — classic late-cycle positioning. Overweight large-cap, dividend-paying stocks (Nifty 50 quality) where SIP money concentrates. Avoid small-cap and mid-cap where retail lump-sum money typically flows. FII outflows (-₹114 cr) mean domestic retail is the marginal buyer — vulnerable to any correction.","pct_10y":85,"pct_note":"SIP AUM at 85th percentile with rising ticket sizes has preceded market peaks in 2021 and 2024. Retail is late to the party.","is_surprise":false}
signal_cards.2026-04-09:4: {"run_date":"2026-04-09","signal_num":4,"signal_theme":"OIL / COMMODITY RISK","status":"risk","title":"Oil Spike Threatens CAD, Inflation Creeps","data_text":"Brent crude at $97.96/bbl (69th percentile, up from $94.75), WTI at $98.91 (82nd percentile). Iron ore surged 50% to $161.91/tonne from $107.83 — China stimulus fears driving commodity hoarding. Copper down 0.7% to $5.718 (94th percentile) — demand destruction signal. India's fuel inflation at -3.78% but turning (up from -4.01%), suggesting pass-through imminent. FAO food index at 128.5 (56th percentile, up from 125.5) — food inflation risk building.","implication":"Oil >$95 adds 40-50bps to India's CAD and 20-30bps to CPI within 2 months. RBI's inflation forecast of 4.6% (up from 2.1%) will prove conservative if Brent stays >$100. Hedge long India equity exposure with short oil or long INR puts. Avoid refiners (IOCL, BPCL) — margins compress. Overweight FMCG with pricing power (HUL, ITC) to offset input cost inflation.","pct_10y":69,"pct_note":"Brent at 69th percentile with rising food inflation has preceded RBI rate hikes in 2022-23. Current pause is data-dependent; oil >$100 forces action.","is_surprise":false}
signal_cards.2026-04-09:5: {"run_date":"2026-04-09","signal_num":5,"signal_theme":"GLOBAL LIQUIDITY","status":"watch","title":"US Growth Stalls, Fed Stays Hawkish","data_text":"US GDP SAAR collapsed to 0.7% from 4.4% — a 370bps miss. US PMI composite at 50.3 (47th percentile, down from 51.9) signals contraction risk. Yet Fed funds rate flat at 3.625% and Fed balance sheet expanding to $6.675T (46th percentile). US 10Y Treasury at 4.26% (down from 4.34%) — yield curve flattening. Nasdaq at 22,635 (100th percentile), S&P 500 at 6,783 (96th percentile) — valuations at extremes despite growth miss.","implication":"US recession risk is rising but Fed won't cut until Q2 2025 at earliest. This creates a liquidity trap: equities are priced for perfection while growth deteriorates. India's FII outflows (-₹114 cr) will accelerate if US yields stay >4.2%. Reduce India equity beta; overweight defensive sectors (pharma, IT services) with US revenue exposure but lower multiple expansion. DXY at 99.04 (46th percentile) — INR has room to weaken if FII flows turn negative.","pct_10y":47,"pct_note":"US PMI at 47th percentile with 0.7% GDP growth is recessionary territory. Last time this happened (2023), Fed cut rates 5 times within 12 months.","is_surprise":false}
signal_cards.2026-04-09:6: {"run_date":"2026-04-09","signal_num":6,"signal_theme":"INR / FX RESERVES","status":"watch","title":"INR Stable But FII Outflows Loom","data_text":"INR/USD at 92.59 (84th percentile, flat from prior day). FII equity net flows at -₹114 cr (49th percentile) — first outflow in 3 months. DII equity net at ₹128 cr (22nd percentile) — domestic support is weak. Gold in INR at ₹2,000/gram (0th percentile, data anomaly but directionally up). RBI's inflation forecast at 4.6% (up from 2.1%) signals no near-term rate cuts, keeping INR supported by carry. However, US 10Y at 4.26% (down from 4.34%) reduces carry appeal.","implication":"INR is supported by RBI's hawkish stance but vulnerable to FII outflows if US growth disappoints further. If FII outflows exceed ₹500 cr/day for 2 consecutive weeks, INR will test 93.50. Hedge long India equity with short INR forwards (sell INR 3-month forwards at 92.80). Watch RBI's next policy meeting (Feb 2025) — if inflation forecast falls below 4%, rate cuts begin and INR weakens 2-3%.","pct_10y":84,"pct_note":"INR at 84th percentile with FII outflows is a warning sign. In 2022, this pattern preceded a 5% INR depreciation within 6 weeks.","is_surprise":false}
signal_cards.2026-04-09:7: {"run_date":"2026-04-09","signal_num":7,"signal_theme":"UNDER THE RADAR","status":"surprise","title":"Office Absorption Decouples From IT Stocks","data_text":"Office absorption in India at 29.9mn sqft (up from 28.2mn sqft, 24th percentile historically) while Nifty IT is flat YTD and trading at 18x forward PE (vs 5-year avg of 16x). Vacancy rates falling to 13.9% from 14.3% — supply-demand tightening. Yet airline passenger traffic collapsed to 35.08mn from 38.59mn (7th percentile) — a proxy for business travel and executive mobility. Rent in Bengaluru up 7.5% YoY to ₹100.6/sqft but Mumbai rents down 10.2% to ₹106/sqft — bifurcation suggests GCC (Global Capability Centres) are the buyer, not traditional IT services.","implication":"IT services are NOT the marginal buyer of office space anymore. GCCs (Google, Amazon, Microsoft, Apple) are consolidating real estate in Tier-1 cities while traditional IT (TCS, Infosys, Wipro) are shrinking headcount. This means office REITs (Embassy, Mindspace) are decoupling from IT stock performance. Overweight office REITs (Embassy REIT at ₹437.42, up 3.8%) and underweight Nifty IT — the narrative of IT-driven office demand is broken. Airline traffic collapse suggests business travel is structurally lower (remote work, AI automation) — office absorption is driven by GCC consolidation, not IT hiring.","pct_10y":24,"pct_note":"Office absorption at 24th percentile with airline traffic at 7th percentile is a regime shift. In 2019-2021, these moved together; now they're diverging, signalling a structural change in who occupies office space.","is_surprise":true}

executive_summary.2026-04-08:1:
{"run_date":"2026-04-08","para_num":1,"para_label":"India Macro Regime","para_html":"

GDP 7.8% with PMI Composite at 57 puts India in unambiguous expansion — 3 of 6 macro dimensions are in growth mode simultaneously. GST collections at ₹2,00,064 Cr YoY alongside PV sales growth of 10.5% confirm demand is broad-based, not sector-specific. CPI at 3.21% with fuel deflation at -3.78% gives RBI room to hold the repo at 5.25% without triggering a policy reversal — duration trades remain intact. Credit growth at 14.3% against deposit growth of 10.9% — CD ratio now 83.04% — is the single structural fault line in an otherwise clean expansion print.

"}
executive_summary.2026-04-08:2:
{"run_date":"2026-04-08","para_num":2,"para_label":"Global Macro Regime","para_html":"

S&P 500 at 6,779 (+2.5%) and Nasdaq at 22,653 (+3.0%, 100th percentile) signal a global risk-on regime that is not priced for disruption. Brent at $94.98/bbl — down 13% from $109.27 — compresses India's import bill and is the single most important tailwind for CAD management this quarter. Copper at $5.766/lb (95th percentile, 10-year range) and iron ore surging to $161.91/t from $108.32 signal industrial demand acceleration, particularly out of China — this is not a soft-landing signal, it is a re-acceleration signal. DXY at 98.84 and US 10Y at 4.246% — both softening — remove the twin headwinds of dollar strength and rate pressure that dominated 2024.

"}
executive_summary.2026-04-08:3:
{"run_date":"2026-04-08","para_num":3,"para_label":"Liquidity Conditions","para_html":"

SIP AUM at ₹16.64 Tr on 9.92 Cr accounts is structural bedrock — a -3.7% MoM dip in SIP inflows to ₹29,845 Cr is noise against 15% YoY growth. Equity MF net inflows accelerated to ₹25,978 Cr (prev: ₹24,029 Cr), and NFO collections exploded to ₹3,955 Cr from ₹806 Cr — DII buying is not slowing, it is rotating into new paper. India VIX collapsing 20.25% to 19.70 from 24.7 — concurrent with US VIX falling to 21.25 — signals a global liquidity unlock that historically precedes 6–8 weeks of sustained equity inflows. FII positioning remains the swing variable; INR stability at 92.59 against a weakening DXY (98.84) removes the currency hedge cost that was suppressing foreign allocation.

"}
executive_summary.2026-04-08:4:
{"run_date":"2026-04-08","para_num":4,"para_label":"Equity + Real Estate Implications","para_html":"

Nifty50 at 23,997 (+3.78%) and Sensex at 77,563 (+3.95%) are pricing the base case — not the bull case — which means reform acceleration or a credit resolution catalyst could drive a further 8–12% re-rating. Real estate presents a structural contradiction: home loan disbursements at ₹5,50,000 Cr are strong, avg rates fell 50 bps to 7.65%, yet RE sales declined 6.7% to 1,01,675 units against an affordability index deteriorating 32% to 0.61 — volume is being bought by rate cuts, not by income growth. Office REITs are the cleaner play: absorption at 83.3 (+13.7%) with vacancy compressing to 15.2% — Embassy REIT at ₹429.85 and Mindspace at ₹449.98 are at 75–79th percentiles, not stretched. Unsold residential inventory at 6,00,000 units caps developer pricing power → residential equity upside is limited without an affordability shock reversal.

"}
executive_summary.2026-04-08:5:
{"run_date":"2026-04-08","para_num":5,"para_label":"Key Risks to Monitor","para_html":"

Ranked by probability × impact: #1 — CD ratio at 83.04% with NBFC credit growing 15–17% on constrained deposits → funding cost spike → NBFC NIM compression → credit crunch cascade (bear scenario trigger, high probability, systemic impact). #2 — Affordability index at 0.61 (+32% deterioration) against 6,00,000 units unsold inventory; any rate reversal → mortgage stress → residential developer defaults (medium probability, concentrated impact). #3 — Copper at 95th percentile and iron ore at $161.91/t signal commodity re-inflation risk; Brent reverting toward $110 — CAD widens $25bn annually → INR pressure → RBI forced tightening → equity multiple compression. Watch the CD ratio print for April — at 83.04%, a further 50–100 bps widening triggers mandatory RBI macro-prudential intervention.

"}
executive_summary.2026-04-09:1:
{"run_date":"2026-04-09","para_num":1,"para_label":"India Macro Regime","para_html":"

7.8% GDP — down from 8.4% — is the kind of number that flatters on the cover page and disappoints on page three: PMI composite fell 240bps to 56.5, core sector decelerated to 2.3% from 4.7%, and capacity utilisation slipped to 74.8% — all pointing to a growth regime that is decelerating, not consolidating. GST at ₹2,00,064 cr is a genuine milestone, but two-wheeler sales flat at 13.4% and airline traffic at the 7th percentile historically suggest the informal economy — 85% of employment — is not sharing in the expansion. RBI's own GDP forecast at 6.9% versus the government's 7.6% FY estimate is a 70bps institutional disagreement that deserves more attention than it is receiving. India's growth is real but narrowing — concentrated in formal-sector services and government-adjacent capex, while the consumption base that sustains it is showing stress at the margins.

"}
executive_summary.2026-04-09:2:
{"run_date":"2026-04-09","para_num":2,"para_label":"Global Macro Regime","para_html":"

US GDP SAAR at 0.7% — down from 4.4%, a 370bps collapse — is the most important global data point this week, yet S&P 500 at the 96th percentile and Nasdaq at the 100th percentile are pricing a soft landing with zero margin for error. US PMI composite at 50.3 (47th percentile) is one bad month from contraction; US core PCE ticking up to 3.1% from 3.0% means the Fed — balance sheet expanding to $6.675T despite flat rates at 3.625% — is caught between a slowing economy and sticky inflation. Iron ore surging 50% to $161.91/tonne on China stimulus expectations — while copper falls 0.7% to $5.718 — is a commodity market sending two contradictory signals simultaneously: hoarding fear versus demand destruction. Brent at $97.96 — every $10 adds $15bn to India's import bill — CAD widens 40bps — INR depreciates 2-3% — imported inflation feeds CPI with a two-quarter lag.

"}
executive_summary.2026-04-09:3:
{"run_date":"2026-04-09","para_num":3,"para_label":"Liquidity Conditions","para_html":"

₹2,59,78 cr in equity MF net inflows and SIP accounts at 1.045 cr — up 5.3% MoM — represent a structural domestic liquidity floor that is deepening, but the composition is shifting: average SIP ticket size at ₹15,900 is rising while NFO collections collapsed to ₹4,979 cr (16th percentile), confirming that retail participation is systematic and SIP-driven, not speculative. FII equity net turning to -₹114 cr — first outflow in three months — with DII support at only ₹128 cr (22nd percentile) means domestic institutional buying is not yet absorbing the FII exit at scale. Corporate bond issuance at ₹9.9 lakh cr — up from ₹8.6 lakh cr — is crowding banks out of credit intermediation, compressing NIM potential even as CD ratio at 82.5% constrains deposit-funded lending. India VIX at 20.4 (16th percentile) is deceptively low given the structural credit stress — the market is not yet pricing the tail risk in the banking system's funding architecture.

"}
executive_summary.2026-04-09:4:
{"run_date":"2026-04-09","para_num":4,"para_label":"Equity + Real Estate Implications","para_html":"

Nifty 50 at 23,775 (76th percentile) and Bank Nifty at 54,822 (90th percentile) are pricing a benign credit cycle — a mispricing, given CD ratio at the 79th percentile and deposit growth structurally lagging credit by 280bps; NIM compression is the second-order effect that bank earnings models have not yet absorbed. Real estate is bifurcating with surgical clarity: office absorption rising to 29.9mn sqft with vacancy falling to 13.9% — driven by GCC demand, not traditional IT services — while residential sales fell to 101.7mn units from 109mn against launches of 126.3mn units, building 600,000 units of unsold inventory. Bengaluru rents up 7.5% to ₹100.6/sqft while Mumbai rents down 10.2% to ₹106/sqft — the divergence maps precisely onto GCC expansion in tech corridors versus traditional financial-sector office demand softening. Embassy REIT at ₹437 (78th percentile) is the cleaner expression of the GCC thesis than Nifty IT at 18x forward PE — 200bps above its five-year average — which is pricing a US tech recovery that US GDP at 0.7% does not support.

"}
executive_summary.2026-04-09:5:
{"run_date":"2026-04-09","para_num":5,"para_label":"Key Risks to Monitor","para_html":"

Ranked by probability × impact: first, fuel pass-through — Brent at $97.96 with fuel deflation narrowing to -3.78% from -4.01% means the CPI suppressor has perhaps two months of runway before administered price revision forces headline above 4.5%, closing the rate-cut window entirely and triggering a bond market reprice. Second, CD ratio crystallisation — at 82.5% for two consecutive months with NBFC credit accelerating to 9.5%, the risk is not a sudden crisis but a slow-motion NIM compression → earnings downgrade → Bank Nifty derating cycle that passive index flows will amplify on the way down. Third, US growth shock transmission — S&P 500 at the 96th percentile with US GDP at 0.7% is a valuation-growth disconnect that historically resolves violently; an equity correction of 10-15% in US markets would trigger FII outflows from India at a moment when DII support is at the 22nd percentile. Fourth, food inflation persistence — CFPI at 3.47% from 2.13% with FAO food index up 3 points to 128.5 creates a political economy constraint on RBI's flexibility that is underappreciated. Watch Friday's RBI liquidity operations and the weekly deposit growth print — if deposit growth does not accelerate toward 12% within the next four weeks, the credit-deposit gap becomes the dominant macro story for Q1 FY27.

"}
